title: Primary, secondary and tertiary education enrollment and completion rates
subtitle: This is given as the gross rate, which includes children of any age entering the level of education; this can result in percentages greater than 100 because children may enter education late or repeat a year.
caption:
Primary, secondary and tertiary education enrollment and completion rates, World

This is given as the gross rate, which includes children of any age entering the level of education; this can result in percentages greater than 100 because children may enter education late or repeat a year.

The line chart tracks global education participation and attainment from 1970 to 2024, measuring gross enrollment and completion rates across four distinct educational stages. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to over 100%, while the horizontal axis spans five decades. Primary enrollment, represented by the top blue line, remains the highest metric throughout the period, starting near 90% in 1970, fluctuating slightly, and ending just above 100% in 2024. Below this, the green line for primary completion begins around 1978 near 78% and climbs steadily to approximately 88% by the end of the timeline. The lower secondary completion rate, shown in purple, starts near 60% in 2000 and rises to match the secondary enrollment rate, both finishing near 78% in 2024. The orange line for secondary enrollment begins at 40% in 1970, dips slightly in the early 1980s, and then follows a consistent upward trajectory to converge with the lower secondary completion figures. The brown line representing tertiary enrollment starts at the bottom, near 10% in 1970, and shows a continuous increase, accelerating after 1990 to reach roughly 43% by 2024. The data shows a clear hierarchy where participation rates decrease as the education level advances, with primary metrics consistently exceeding secondary and tertiary figures. All visible series display an upward trend over the observed period, with the gap between enrollment and completion narrowing for secondary education in recent years.
